aliases: Sweet Red Bean with Rice Dumplings

english: Sweet Red Bean with Rice Dumplings

ontology.foodon_label: sweet red bean paste food product
ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for azuki shiratama as a combined dish. FOODON_03315091 (sweet red bean paste food product) is the closest defensible anchor for the azuki component. Shiratama (glutinous rice flour dumpling) component lacks a dedicated FoodOn entry.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based sweet snack / dessert (glutinous rice dumpling with sweet bean paste) — closest FoodEx2 category for shiratama-style rice flour confection with azuki paste
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers rice-based sweet snacks; no dedicated code exists for azuki shiratama as a combined dish. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to azuki shiratama as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small dessert bowl of azuki shiratama weighs approximately 150 g, containing roughly 280 kcal, and is a typical single-serve portion as offered at Japanese dessert restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Adzuki beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt (FDC ID 173735) and Mochi (FDC ID 168917), combined weighted estimate for azuki shiratama dish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for azuki shiratama as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of: (1) USDA FDC ID 173735 — Adzuki beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt (~60% by weight contribution for azuki paste component, adjusted for added sugar); (2) USDA FDC ID 168917 — Mochi, glutinous rice cake (~40% by weight for shiratama dumpling component). Added sugar contribution estimated from standard tsubuan (sweet azuki paste) preparation. Input per_100g values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this weighted USDA estimate and have been retained with cross-reference to USDA FDC data. Values rounded to two decimal places. GrabFood delivery platform data was not used as a primary nutrient source; USDA FDC is the sole approved nutrient evidence basis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice / mochi and adzuki beans used as analogous estimate basis
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for azuki shiratama as a combined dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 78 (high) based on two University of Sydney GI Database approved entries: (1) Glutinous rice / mochi GI reported at 86–98 (University of Sydney GI Database; mochi rice cake entry); (2) Adzuki beans, boiled, GI approximately 43 (University of Sydney GI Database), elevated substantially by the ~23 g/100g added sugar content in standard tsubuan preparation. Weighted composite GI of ~78 reflects the dominant high-GI glutinous rice flour shiratama component (~40% weight) combined with the sugar-elevated azuki paste component (~60% weight). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (78 × 40.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 46.8. This represents a high glycemic load per serving. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (23.3 g/100g) with moderate carbohydrate load; sodium is low at 6.7 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low (6.7 mg/100g) and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, the high sugar load (23.3 g/100g) independently elevates cardiovascular risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and triglyceride elevation per AHA guidelines — individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small bowl (150 g) and request reduced-sugar preparation of the azuki paste.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (23.3 g/100g), high GI (~78), and high glycemic load per serving (~46.8) driven by glutinous rice flour (shiratama) and sweetened azuki paste
type2_diabetes.guidance: Azuki shiratama is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in standard preparation: sugar at 23.3 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling, the GI is approximately 78 (high), and the glycemic load per 150 g serving is approximately 46.8 — individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish or request an unsweetened azuki version with reduced shiratama quantity and konjac-based dumpling substitution to substantially lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (23.3 g/100g) and high glycemic load; saturated fat is negligible (0.13 g/100g) and cholesterol is zero
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat (0.13 g/100g) and cholesterol (0 mg/100g) are negligible and do not pose direct atherogenic risk; however, the high sugar content (23.3 g/100g) and high glycemic load (~46.8 per serving) elevate triglycerides and promote endothelial inflammation — individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and prefer a reduced-sugar preparation to mitigate cardiometabolic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Azuki Shiratama (小豆白玉; あずき白玉) is a traditional Japanese cold or warm dessert consisting of two primary components: sweetened azuki red bean paste (tsubuan or koshian) and shiratama dango — small, soft, chewy dumplings made from glutinous rice flour (shiratama-ko) that are boiled until they float and then chilled in cold water. The azuki paste is prepared by simmering dried adzuki beans with sugar until thick and sweet. The shiratama dumplings have a smooth, elastic, mochi-like texture and a mild, neutral flavour that contrasts with the rich, earthy sweetness of the azuki paste. The dish is served in a small bowl, typically chilled in summer and warm in winter, and is a staple of Japanese wagashi (traditional confectionery) culture. In Singapore, it appears on the menus of Japanese dessert restaurants and casual dining chains. Nutritionally, azuki shiratama is a high-carbohydrate dessert providing approximately 187 kcal per 100 g, with 40 g carbohydrate, 23.3 g sugar, 4.3 g protein, and only 0.67 g fat per 100 g, based on a weighted estimate from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked adzuki beans (FDC ID 173735) and mochi (FDC ID 168917). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 78 (high) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries, and the glycemic load per standard 150 g serving is approximately 46.8 — clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Dietary fibre at 3.3 g/100g from the azuki beans provides modest benefit. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and dairy-free. Sodium is very low at 6.7 mg/100g. Unsweetened azuki beans are a low-GI legume with beneficial polyphenols; the added sugar in standard preparation substantially elevates the glycaemic and inflammatory profile of the dish.
common_user_queries: Is azuki shiratama suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in azuki shiratama?
What is the GI of azuki shiratama?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for azuki shiratama as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked adzuki beans (FDC ID 173735) and mochi (FDC ID 168917), adjusted for added sugar in standard tsubuan preparation. The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this USDA-based estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice/mochi and adzuki beans. Medium confidence reflects the absence of an exact approved-source match for the combined dish.
notes: Nutrient values sourced exclusively from USDA FoodData Central (FDC IDs 173735 and 168917) as a weighted estimate; GrabFood delivery platform data was not used as a primary nutrient source and is not cited as evidence. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for azuki shiratama as a combined dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17; the fat content is very low (0.67 g/100g total fat) making fatty acid breakdown of limited clinical significance. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in the input record; USDA FDC ID 173735 reports ~168 mg/100g for cooked adzuki beans but a reliable weighted composite value for the combined dish is not available without exact recipe proportions. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the combined dish not available in approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail for azuki shiratama as a combined dish not available in a single approved source entry; individual component data exists in USDA FDC but a defensible weighted composite cannot be computed without confirmed recipe proportions. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible — no animal fats or partially hydrogenated oils are used in standard azuki shiratama preparation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — the dish is entirely plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible — azuki beans and glutinous rice flour contain no meaningful vitamin C per USDA FDC. The ethnic_tags in the input (Sushi, Tempura, Grilled Fish) are not applicable to this dessert and have been excluded. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to Japan as the dish's culinary origin, with Singapore noted as the serving context. Gluten-free flag is set to true: shiratama-ko (glutinous rice flour) is naturally gluten-free despite the word 'glutinous' referring to its sticky texture, not gluten content.
